title: Comments
description: Single-line, multi-line, and Javadoc comments — how to leave notes in code the compiler ignores.
objectives: Recognize Javadoc comments (/** */) and what they're for
Use comments to explain why, not just what

sections.text: Text in source code that the compiler completely ignores — written for humans reading the code, not for the computer.
You've already seen comments in earlier lessons, like // valid next to a variable declaration, without it being explained. Today we cover the three comment styles Java supports.
Everything after // on that line is ignored by the compiler, whether it's the whole line or just the end of it.
Everything between /* and */ is ignored, even across multiple lines. The leading * on each inner line is just a common style convention — it has no special meaning to the compiler.

Multi-line comments cannot be nested. /* outer /* inner */ still-outer? */ does not work the way you might expect — the first */ closes the comment early, and still-outer? */ becomes actual code, which usually breaks compilation.

This example uses a method (calculateArea) and @param/@return tags — concepts not covered yet. We only preview Javadoc comments here because they look similar to /* */ comments and beginners often confuse the two. Full method syntax is covered in the Methods module.
A specially formatted comment starting with /** that documentation-generation tools can read to automatically produce reference documentation for your code.

sections.title: The three comment styles
rows: Short notes, one line at a time
Documentation meant to be read by tools that generate reference docs

sections.text: A comment that just repeats what the code already says isn't useful. A good comment explains why something is done, especially when the reason isn't obvious from the code alone.

As your programs grow, resist the urge to comment every single line. Clear variable and method names (like totalPrice instead of x) often make comments unnecessary — save comments for the parts that genuinely need explaining.
Comments are also used to temporarily disable a line of code without deleting it — often while testing or debugging.

sections.code:
int total = price * quantity;
// int total = price * quantity * 2; // old calculation, kept for reference

sections.text: This is fine while actively working on something, but leaving lots of commented-out old code lying around long-term usually just clutters a file — it's generally better to remove code you no longer need once you're confident you won't need it back.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Trying to nest /* */ comments inside each other — the first closing */ ends the comment early.
Writing comments that just restate the code (// increment i by 1 next to i++;) instead of explaining why.
Confusing /** */ (Javadoc, read by documentation tools) with a plain /* */ comment.
Leaving large blocks of commented-out old code permanently in a file instead of removing what's no longer needed.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What are the three types of comments in Java?
questions.answer: Single-line comments (//), multi-line comments (/* */), and Javadoc comments (/** */), which documentation-generation tools can read to produce reference docs.

questions.question: Can multi-line comments be nested in Java?
questions.answer: No. The first */ encountered closes the comment, so nested /* */ pairs don't work as expected and often cause a compile error in the leftover text.

questions.question: Do comments affect a compiled Java program's behavior?
questions.answer: No. Comments are completely ignored by the compiler — they exist purely for humans reading the source code.

questions.question: What makes a comment useful rather than clutter?
questions.answer: A useful comment explains why something is done, especially when the reason isn't obvious — comments that just restate what the code already clearly says add little value.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Java supports three comment styles: // for single-line notes, /* */ for multi-line explanations, and /** */ (Javadoc) for documentation that tooling can extract into reference docs. Comments are completely ignored by the compiler and exist purely for human readers. Multi-line comments can't be nested, and the most useful comments explain why code does something rather than restating what it obviously already does.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: // = one line. /* */ = a block. /** */ = documentation (the extra * is what makes it "special"). Comments explain WHY, the code already shows WHAT.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What are the three comment styles in Java?
Why is a comment that just restates the code not very useful?
